name: The Needles
short_description: BLM rockhounding near Moab featuring colorful agates, jasper, and quartz found among iconic desert rock formations. Public land with no permit required.
description:
The Needles area in Grand County, Utah offers accessible rockhounding on public BLM land near Moab's famous red rock landscape. This desert site is known for scattered agate nodules, jasper specimens, and colorful quartz fragments found in washes and along hillsides. The distinctive rock formations that give the area its name create a visually stunning backdrop while you search for specimens.

The geology here reflects Utah's rich basin-and-range province character, where Paleozoic sedimentary rocks and younger desert deposits have weathered to expose mineral-rich layers. Agate and jasper deposits formed through silica-rich groundwater percolating through ancient rock formations over millions of years, depositing the colorful chalcedony varieties you find today.

The site works well for rockhounds of all skill levels. Beginners can spot specimens along washes after rain, while experienced collectors explore higher outcrops and hillsides. The terrain is rugged but walkable, with good surface finds that don't require heavy digging.

Visitors should expect a typical high desert environment with minimal vegetation and excellent visibility. The area sees seasonal use from both rockhounds and outdoor enthusiasts, so early morning visits often yield the best finds. Water and supplies are available in Moab, about 30 miles away.
history: The Needles area sits within the Colorado Plateau province, where Paleozoic and Mesozoic sedimentary rocks were uplifted and eroded by the Colorado River system. Silica-rich fluids percolating through these ancient formations during the Cenozoic era deposited the agate and jasper nodules found here today, while the distinctive needle-like rock spires formed through differential erosion of resistant sandstone layers.
tips: Search washes and arroyos after rain events, as water movement exposes fresh material and concentrates specimens. Look for colorful agate nodules the size of walnuts to fists, often with chalcedony banding visible on broken surfaces. Bring a geology hammer and hand lens to identify specimens in place before collecting. Morning light casts shadows that make it easier to spot translucent pieces against the tan desert soil.
terrain_notes: Elevation around 5,000 feet. Rugged, rolling desert terrain with sparse vegetation and exposed red sandstone outcrops. Washes and gullies cut through sagebrush slopes. Walking distance from parking ranges 0.5 to 2 miles depending on chosen collecting area. Footing is uneven with loose rock scattered throughout; sturdy hiking boots strongly recommended. No shade or water on site.
best_season: October - April. The high desert heat makes summer (June-August) challenging, with afternoon temperatures exceeding 100 degrees Fahrenheit. Spring and fall offer ideal conditions with cool mornings, excellent visibility, and higher chances of finding fresh material exposed by winter and spring rains.
written_directions: From Moab, take US 191 north for approximately 30 miles. Look for BLM access points and pullouts in the high desert terrain east of the highway. The Needles area is accessed via rough dirt roads branching east from US 191; a high-clearance vehicle is recommended. GPS coordinates (38.685353, -109.916518) point directly to the main rockhounding zone. Allow 45 minutes to 1 hour driving time from Moab.
road_conditions: Rough dirt road with washboard texture and potential high-clearance requirement. Road conditions vary seasonally and after rain events. Standard passenger vehicles may struggle; high-clearance or all-wheel-drive recommended.
parking_notes: Multiple informal pullouts and parking areas along BLM access roads. No designated lot; park off-road in flat areas away from active roads. Capacity varies by location. Some areas may have better parking than others depending on seasonal use.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 pounds per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or mechanical tools allowed. Pack out all trash. Stay on designated roads and respect any seasonal closures. Collect only loose material; do not hammer at rock outcrops or remove samples from intact formations.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Pack out all trash
Stay on designated roads
Collect loose material only
nearest_services: Moab, approximately 30 miles south. Full services including gas, food, water, and lodging available. Equipment and supplies can be purchased in town.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky terrain with no wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots required. Rough dirt road to access area may be impassable to standard vehicles, especially after rain. Once parked, walking areas involve uneven slopes, loose rock, and arroyos. Moderate physical fitness recommended. Heat exposure significant in summer months.

faq.question: What types of rocks and minerals can you find at The Needles near Moab, Utah?
faq.answer: The Needles BLM area is best known for colorful agate nodules with chalcedony banding, red and orange jasper specimens, and quartz fragments. Most pieces are fist-sized or smaller. You may also find pieces of petrified wood and chert scattered in washes. The high desert deposits here have weathered to expose these secondary minerals formed from ancient silica-rich groundwater.

faq.question: Do you need a permit to rockhound at The Needles in Grand County, Utah?
faq.answer: No permit is required at The Needles. This is BLM public land where recreational rockhounding is allowed under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). You can collect up to 25 pounds per day for personal use without any permit or fee. Commercial collection is not permitted.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at The Needles, Utah?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. Summer heat in this high desert location exceeds 100 degrees Fahrenheit, making it uncomfortable and potentially dangerous. Spring and fall offer perfect conditions with cool mornings, excellent visibility for spotting specimens, and higher chances of finding fresh material exposed by seasonal rains. Winter visits are possible but bring extra water and sun protection.

faq.question: How far is The Needles rockhounding site from Moab, and what kind of vehicle do you need?
faq.answer: The Needles is approximately 30 miles north of Moab via US 191. A high-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicle is strongly recommended, as access roads are rough dirt tracks with washboard conditions. Standard passenger cars may struggle, especially after rain. Budget 45 minutes to 1 hour for the drive from Moab depending on road conditions.

faq.question: Is The Needles rockhounding site suitable for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, The Needles works well for beginners and families with kids ages 8 and up. Specimens are often visible along washes without heavy digging required. However, the terrain is rugged with loose rock and uneven footing, so sturdy hiking boots are essential. Bring plenty of water, sun protection, and supervise children closely. The remote location and heat make it important to be well-prepared.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should you bring when rockhounding at The Needles near Moab?
faq.answer: Bring a geology hammer, hand lens, heavy gloves, and sturdy hiking boots. A backpack, water bottles (at least 2-3 liters per person), sunscreen, hat, and high-energy snacks are essential for this remote high-desert site. A field guide to Utah minerals helps with identification. GPS and a paper map are useful since cell service is spotty. A bucket or collection bag helps carry your 25-pound daily limit.

meta_description: Find agate, jasper, and quartz at The Needles BLM site near Moab, Utah. No permit required, 25 lbs/day limit. Perfect for beginners.
